title: Happy Ending

jokes.text: Who died? Did Patrick die?
jokes.setup: David is startled awake and his first panicked thought is that someone has died
jokes.punchline: He immediately and specifically names Patrick — his fiancé — as the most likely deceased, revealing both his catastrophizing nature and deep attachment
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific: David's immediate assumption of worst-case scenario AND his specific fixation on Patrick tells us everything about him in two lines. The economy is excellent — pure David Rose panic in six words.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? What happened? Is Patrick dead?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, he's fine. I just wanted to tell you something.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you can't just wake someone up like that. You could kill them.

jokes.text: A hiccup. / What hiccup? Who's hiccupping?
jokes.setup: Moira uses the obscure medical term 'singultus' to describe a problem with the wedding
jokes.punchline: David has no idea what 'singultus' means and assumes someone is literally hiccupping, leading to confused panic
jokes.explanation: A perfect Moira joke — she uses an absurdly elevated word when 'hiccup' would do, and David's literal misinterpretation ('Who's hiccupping?') compounds the comedy. Character integration is exceptional: only Moira would say 'singultus' and only David would respond this way.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm feeling rather pellucid today.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, so you're transparent.

jokes.text: Oh. Imagine. (in response to David saying something really, really bad happened)
jokes.setup: David dramatically declares that his mind went to 'some kind of unthinkable tragedy, something really, really bad'
jokes.punchline: Moira's dry 'Oh. Imagine.' — confirming the wedding IS in fact a disaster without saying so
jokes.explanation: Two words doing enormous work. Moira's withering economy here is peak character comedy — she confirms the disaster without drama, which is ironic given that she IS drama. The misdirection is subtle: we expect her to deny it, she sardonically confirms it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Bob's Garage is located in a low-lying basin, which means it experiences its own microclimate. During the spring equinox, there's a convergence of warm and cold fronts that creates a perpetual mist. It's essentially a swamp.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a parking lot.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A swamp, David.

jokes.text: Oh my God. To where? Bob's Garage?
jokes.setup: The family reveals they need to change the wedding venue due to storm damage
jokes.punchline: David's first panicked alternative is 'Bob's Garage' — a hilariously specific, low-status guess that reveals his social snobbery
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Bob's Garage' is the joke — David doesn't say 'somewhere random,' he names the actual worst-case local option. It's character comedy that works because it establishes the stakes of David's aesthetic anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's like The Handmaid's Tale, except everyone's fertile and no one's being punished.

jokes.text: No, his roof was no match for the cumulus clouds.
jokes.setup: David suggests Bob's Garage as a backup venue
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses it with meteorological precision — 'cumulus clouds' — as if this is normal vocabulary for explaining why a garage is unavailable
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira elevated diction deployed for mundane information. 'Cumulus clouds' instead of 'rain' is a perfect character beat — she can't help being theatrical even in a crisis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're a wedding officiant arriving in a hot air balloon?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm also a haikuist, David.

jokes.text: I've woken up in a 'Black Mirror' episode.
jokes.setup: The cascade of wedding disasters keeps mounting
jokes.punchline: David frames his real-world stress through a prestige TV dystopia reference, which is exactly how a self-dramatizing millennial would process chaos
jokes.explanation: A functional pop-culture joke that works because it's character-appropriate for David, but it leans on name-dropping rather than pure wit. Serviceable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, I would be honored to officiate your wedding.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

jokes.text: This might be a stupid question because there is like literally nothing you can do about it, but why didn't you guys get a tent?
jokes.setup: The family is in crisis mode about the rained-out outdoor wedding
jokes.punchline: Alexis's pre-emptive self-awareness ('this might be a stupid question') followed immediately by asking the most unhelpful possible question
jokes.explanation: Alexis's self-aware obliviousness is pitch-perfect: she knows she's being useless and does it anyway. The structure is strong — the disclaimer makes the question funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I've officiated seventeen weddings. Seventeen! And do you know how many of those couples are still together? Well, I don't know the exact number, but I'm quite certain it's more than zero. Plus, I once correctly identified a Wheel of Fortune puzzle at a celebrity wedding before the bride and groom did. It was 'LOVE IS IN THE AIR.' I solved it in my head. So clearly, I have a gift for this sort of thing.

jokes.text: Because I wanted the wood-fired pizza oven and we couldn't afford both.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks why they didn't get a tent for the outdoor wedding
jokes.punchline: David admits he prioritized a pizza oven over weather protection — a perfectly David Rose luxury-over-practicality choice
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent joke. The specific detail — wood-fired pizza oven — is not just any luxury, it's the most David Rose luxury: artisanal, food-related, impractical. It instantly reveals everything about his priorities while being genuinely funny. The economy is superb; one sentence does enormous character work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, we have money now, so we can just buy a new wedding.

jokes.text: What happened to Fabian?! / He cancelled because of the rain. / What kind of adult man rides a penny-farthing? / He's a haikuist!
jokes.setup: The officiant has cancelled due to the rain
jokes.punchline: David's bafflement that the officiant (Fabian) rides a penny-farthing is immediately explained by Moira as 'He's a haikuist!' — as if this explains everything
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent absurdist exchange. 'What kind of adult man rides a penny-farthing?' is a great line on its own, but 'He's a haikuist!' as if that's a complete explanation is inspired. The two details together — penny-farthing + haikuist — paint a vivid portrait of a person we never see. High originality for the specificity of the absurdist combo.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't do this anymore! I just— I can't!

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It's okay, it's okay.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, babe, you're fine.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, you don't understand. I need to leave. Right now.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, go ahead.

jokes.text: Well, David, I-I-I could easily step in here. I have a lot to say about my son getting married.
jokes.setup: The officiant has cancelled and someone needs to step in
jokes.punchline: Johnny volunteers with heartfelt confidence, but David immediately shuts him down before he can finish the sentence
jokes.explanation: A functional setup for the better joke that follows. The comedy is mostly in the anticipation of David's dismissal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Look, I never said an indoor wedding was a bad idea. I said that outdoor weddings were better for the aesthetic, which they are. But that doesn't mean indoor weddings are bad. They're just... different. And different isn't worse, it's just different.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So you're saying...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm saying that if we had done it indoors, we would have had an indoor wedding, which would have been fine. But we did it outdoors, and it was also fine. So really, both scenarios were equally fine, which means I was right either way.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's not how being right works.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it is now.

jokes.text: You can barely get through this without crying, so that's a no.
jokes.setup: Johnny volunteers to officiate the wedding
jokes.punchline: David coldly and correctly points out that Johnny is already too emotional to get through a speech — delivered as a matter-of-fact assessment, not cruelty
jokes.explanation: Sharp and affectionate simultaneously — which is very Schitt's Creek. David isn't being cruel; he's making a practical assessment that also reveals Johnny's emotional tenderness. The 'that's a no' delivery is perfectly brusque.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The carpet is a sort of... burnt sienna, which photographs terribly. And in the context of these photos, it's going to read as just a sort of... depressing rust.

jokes.text: A note, I did bring the house down at Pat Sajak's first wedding. 'Can I buy a vow.' I had the studio audience wrapped around my finger.
jokes.setup: David has shot down Johnny for officiating; Moira inserts herself into the conversation
jokes.punchline: Moira claims she attended Pat Sajak's FIRST wedding (implying multiple) and delivered the line 'Can I buy a vow' — a Wheel of Fortune wordplay pun — to a studio audience
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. Multiple layers working simultaneously: (1) 'Pat Sajak's FIRST wedding' implies there were multiple, a throwaway detail that's hilarious; (2) 'Can I buy a vow' is a genuinely clever Wheel of Fortune pun that fits Moira's showbiz world; (3) 'I had the studio audience wrapped around my finger' — at a wedding — is pure absurdist Moira logic. Only Moira Rose could deliver this line. High originality, exceptional character integration, and the pun is actually witty rather than groan-worthy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I know you. By next Tuesday, you're going to be wound so tight that you'll need a deep tissue massage just to remember what relaxation feels like.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's fair.

jokes.text: What if the sun comes out and everything's fine. / [02:00] Fuck! Fuck! Fuck! Fuck!
jokes.setup: Alexis optimistically suggests the sun might come out; everyone turns to look outside
jokes.punchline: Immediately, the full extent of the storm damage is visible — multiple characters curse in unison, Moira/Johnny/David all saying 'Fuck!' repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Perfect sitcom structure: optimistic setup, instant brutal punchline. The fact that even prim Moira joins in the 'Fuck!'s is the extra comedic layer. The economy is exceptional — the setup is one hopeful line, the punchline is pure reaction. High impact because it's a release of tension that's been building.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How did you even manage to pull this together on such short notice?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't. I planned this weeks ago. I know you, David. I knew you'd have a breakdown eventually, so I just... prepared for it.

jokes.text: We should've gotten a tent. / I didn't need pizza that badly. / Wow, let's not get crazy.
jokes.setup: After seeing the storm damage, the reality of the tent/pizza oven decision lands
jokes.punchline: Patrick generously concedes ('I didn't need pizza that badly') but David immediately backtracks ('let's not get crazy') — defending his pizza oven even now
jokes.explanation: An excellent callback that deepens the original joke. Patrick being willing to give up the pizza is sweet and funny; David refusing to concede even in disaster is peak David Rose. 'Let's not get crazy' lands as a big laugh because it's so perfectly his priorities laid bare. The callback structure is tight and the character integration couldn't be better.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You've eaten five bagels in the last two days.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I didn't intend to.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You didn't intend to eat five bagels?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: You said indoor weddings are tacky. / No. I said most weddings are tacky. And they often take place indoors.
jokes.setup: Patrick points out that David had previously dismissed indoor weddings as 'tacky'
jokes.punchline: David makes a pedantic distinction — 'most weddings are tacky' and they happen to often be indoors — refusing to admit any logical inconsistency
jokes.explanation: David's logical gymnastics to avoid admitting he was wrong are perfectly executed. The distinction he draws is absurd but delivered with complete sincerity. Strong character integration — this is exactly how David Rose defends himself.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, I've been working on some material for the wedding. I thought I'd incorporate some Wheel of Fortune references.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But let's be honest, if Vanna White wouldn't laugh at it, then nobody's going to laugh at it.

jokes.text: Besides those carpets do not photograph well.
jokes.setup: David agrees not to get married in Roland's living room but needs a practical reason
jokes.punchline: His stated objection is purely aesthetic — the carpets won't photograph well — not that it's someone's living room or undignified
jokes.explanation: A sharp one-liner that is pure David. The objection is photographic, not ethical or social. Perfect economy and high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I guess that didn't land. You know, I've bombed in front of tougher crowds than this. I once told a joke to Vanna White and she didn't even turn a letter.

jokes.text: I have arranged for a massage to chill you out a little bit... Oh no, all this was planned weeks ago. Just had a hunch you wouldn't handle stress well today.
jokes.setup: Patrick reveals he pre-booked a massage for David's wedding morning
jokes.punchline: He admits it was planned 'weeks ago' — knowing in advance that David would be a disaster — which is a loving but devastating assessment of David's personality
jokes.explanation: Works on two levels: it's funny that Patrick planned for David's meltdown in advance, and it's sweet that he did. The comedy is in the casual delivery of 'just had a hunch' — understated and affectionate.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm actually really upset about this.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, look at you getting in touch with your feelings. How very Oprah of you.

jokes.text: You had like three bagels in half an hour. / They were a mistake.
jokes.setup: David claims he's been handling the stress 'just fine'
jokes.punchline: Patrick casually notes the three bagels consumed in thirty minutes; David's resigned 'they were a mistake' is the perfect admission
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. 'Three bagels in half an hour' is perfectly specific and visual; 'they were a mistake' is one of those deadpan admissions that lands perfectly because it's so resigned and self-aware. The economy is superb — four words that contain a whole character moment. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm actually really happy here. I love this town, I love the people, and I love our life.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's just Stockholm Syndrome, darling.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, it's not.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Mmm, yes it is. We were taken hostage by circumstance, and now we're identifying with our captors. It's a very common response to prolonged trauma.

jokes.text: Don't neglect the lighting. / Okay. Love you, too.
jokes.setup: Patrick says goodbye and 'love you' as David is being sent off to his massage
jokes.punchline: David's parting instruction is about wedding venue lighting, not an 'I love you back' — until a beat later when he adds it almost as an afterthought
jokes.explanation: A nice beat that shows David's priorities (logistics first, love second) while also being genuinely warm. The comedic rhythm depends on the pause between the lighting note and 'Love you, too.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know what? I love you, Mom.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, darling...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Boop.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Did you just... boop my nose?

jokes.text: 'Hello, you. Are-are we all ready to spin the wheel of life today?'
jokes.setup: Johnny is practicing his officiant speech
jokes.punchline: His opening line is a clumsy Wheel of Fortune reference ('spin the wheel of life') that he's clearly proud of, directly mirroring Moira's earlier 'Can I buy a vow' joke but with far less polish
jokes.explanation: Works as a callback to Moira's slicker 'Can I buy a vow' — Johnny's version is clunkier and less polished, which is itself the joke. His self-conscious stammer ('Are-are we') shows he knows it's not quite landing. Good character contrast between the two parents' comedic sensibilities.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you're saying it's just muscle tension?

dialogue.speaker: Masseur
dialogue.line: Yes, that's what I'm saying.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You seemed very quick to dismiss that theory.

dialogue.speaker: Masseur
dialogue.line: Because it's correct.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but you didn't even consider my alternative hypothesis.

jokes.text: Even Vanna White didn't laugh at that one.
jokes.setup: Johnny apparently delivers the line 'Are we ready to spin the wheel of life today?' — a Wheel of Fortune riff echoing Moira's earlier 'Can I buy a vow' joke
jokes.punchline: Johnny's self-deprecating review of his own joke: 'Even Vanna White didn't laugh at that one' — acknowledging it fell flat before anyone else could
jokes.explanation: A callback to the Wheel of Fortune theme and a self-aware meta-joke about joke quality. Johnny's self-deprecation is endearing and the Vanna White specificity is strong. Works as a standalone and as a callback.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I know the florist. I can get you extra flowers. We go way back.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh. Oh!

jokes.text: Alexis, have I ever told you how bewitching you are. [pause] It's a compliment.
jokes.setup: Moira unexpectedly compliments Alexis on an emotional morning
jokes.punchline: The pause and then 'It's a compliment' — implying Alexis's reaction was confusion or suspicion, which Moira has to clarify
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the implied reaction: Moira's compliments are so rare or so oddly worded that Alexis doesn't know what to do with one. 'It's a compliment' is the perfect clarification from a mother whose praise is historically unrecognizable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David will take one look at that place and say 'I'm not getting married in a building that smells like a wet dog that's been dead for three weeks.'

jokes.text: He can be very cruel, but it's all fear-based, Dear. So please don't take it personally.
jokes.setup: Alexis is emotional and mentions David; Moira thinks she's upset by something David said
jokes.punchline: Moira's assessment of her son — 'very cruel, but it's all fear-based' — delivered as unsolicited parenting insight with therapist-speak vocabulary
jokes.explanation: Moira casually calling David 'very cruel' while trying to comfort Alexis is darkly funny. The 'fear-based' psychology speak is peak Moira — she knows the right words but deploys them in the wrong order.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I went for a massage last week. It was wonderful. The masseuse asked if I wanted the happy ending.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, that's—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I said yes, obviously. And you know what? She brought me a cookie.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: A cookie.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. It was a very happy ending.

jokes.text: Unless it was the year I booked that international campaign for Looky-Loo Binoculars.
jokes.setup: Moira claims she and Alexis never went a full year without seeing each other
jokes.punchline: She immediately walks it back with the caveat of the Looky-Loo Binoculars campaign — an absurdly named, clearly low-rent international gig that Moira treats as prestigious
jokes.explanation: The invented brand name 'Looky-Loo Binoculars' is inspired — it's hilariously tacky for something Moira would call 'international.' The specificity and absurdity of the detail is excellent. High originality and perfect character integration: only Moira's career would have this particular low point treated as a high point.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have a mood board.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Of course you do.

jokes.text: Well, I'm happy you and your feeling are so well acquainted. But you wouldn't be the first hostage to fall in love with their captor.
jokes.setup: Alexis says she's almost glad the family lost their money because it brought them together
jokes.punchline: Moira responds with Stockholm Syndrome as the explanation for Alexis's attachment to the family — wickedly dark while still being a form of love
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The Stockholm Syndrome reference is both perfectly mean and perfectly loving — Moira's way of acknowledging that their family bond is real while refusing to be sentimental. The setup line ('I'm happy you and your feeling are so well acquainted') is itself beautifully constructed. Dark, witty, specific, quotable — this is exceptional Schitt's Creek writing.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Wait, what? No, no, no. You didn't... tell me you didn't actually...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, it's not what you think—

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Oh my God. OH MY GOD. I can't believe you would do this to me. To us. I thought we had something special here!

jokes.text: Thank you, I needed a moment of levity today.
jokes.setup: Moira has just told Alexis she has Stockholm Syndrome
jokes.punchline: Alexis thanks her for it — treating the Stockholm Syndrome quip as 'levity' — revealing either her own dark humor or her complete desensitization to Moira
jokes.explanation: A nice button on joke #24 — Alexis's equanimity about the Stockholm comment reveals her character. Works as a topper.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Are you seriously trying to tell me you didn't know?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Know what? Patrick, I have no idea what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You're going to stand there and lie to my face?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not lying. I genuinely don't know what you mean. Can you please just tell me what this is about?

jokes.text: I think you're very good at this, or the half pill I just took kicked in. Or both.
jokes.setup: David is getting a massage and feeling calm for the first time all morning
jokes.punchline: He acknowledges he can't tell if the masseur is skilled or if the pill he took is responsible — delivered with perfect 'or both' hedging
jokes.explanation: Funny oversharing from David — 'the half pill I just took' is casually dropped as if it's entirely normal. Good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Wait, so you were getting a massage?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Patrick. A therapeutic massage. For my back.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: And Ray was there?

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: I was documenting it for the business Instagram.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ray, why would you—

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: I got some great shots of the oils, the ambiance...

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So what's the problem?

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Well, I may have accidentally focused on David's face the entire time instead of the therapeutic aspects.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ray, you filmed me looking like I was in pain for forty-five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: It was very dramatic. I thought it was working.

jokes.text: Well, that's good, because your neck is like a block of granite. / Okay. Are we sure that's not just muscle? / Yes. / All right, you answered that too quickly.
jokes.setup: The masseur comments on David's neck tension
jokes.punchline: David's hopeful 'are we sure that's not just muscle?' is immediately shut down; his 'you answered that too quickly' is the perfect wounded comeback
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure (insult/defense/shutdown/comeback) is efficient and funny. 'You answered that too quickly' is a very good line — David's wounded vanity expressed with characteristic indignation.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I arranged for the caterer to come early, set up in the back, and then slip out discreetly once everything was ready.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, that's... that's not what that sounds like.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What? I just wanted everything to be perfect and out of sight.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I understand what you *meant*, but telling someone to 'slip out discreetly through the back' is a very different sentence.

jokes.text: Our living room is yours, Johnny. And we still have some baby sprinkle decorations gathering dust.
jokes.setup: Johnny is asking the group not to suggest Roland's living room as a venue
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately offers exactly that — and sweetens the deal with leftover 'baby sprinkle decorations'
jokes.explanation: Decent joke — 'baby sprinkle decorations gathering dust' is a funny specific detail. Roland's obliviousness to being pre-rejected is good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You know, when I think about how we met, and everything we've been through, and now we're here getting married... it's like we're living in a sitcom.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We are living in a sitcom.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Oh, as David's Maid-of-Honour, I'm gonna shut that down on his behalf.
jokes.setup: Roland offers his living room as the wedding venue
jokes.punchline: Stevie, as Maid-of-Honour, officially vetoes it on David's behalf — the formality of invoking her title to reject a living room wedding is the joke
jokes.explanation: The procedural invocation of her Maid-of-Honour title to veto a living room venue is a nice joke. Stevie's deadpan authority is well-deployed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was impressed.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You were impressed? What are you, the OJ prosecutor? I'm not taking any more questions.

jokes.text: Ray, you don't need to be documenting this. / I was told to capture the day. / Ray, I don't think this is a part of the day that anybody is gonna wanna remember. / Can you say that again for the camera?
jokes.setup: Johnny tells the wedding videographer Ray to stop filming the chaotic planning session
jokes.punchline: Ray, oblivious or deliberately ironic, asks Johnny to repeat the statement about not wanting it filmed — for the camera
jokes.explanation: A well-structured meta-joke: the request not to film is itself filmed. Ray's cheerful obtuseness ('Can you say that again for the camera?') is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I just told them to make sure the masseuse knows to focus on my lower back and... oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I said 'work it out thoroughly' and 'don't be shy about going deep.'

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's... that's not what I meant at all.

jokes.text: Johnny, when I say I know the florist, I mean, I know the florist.
jokes.setup: Johnny dismisses Ronnie's offer to get extra flowers from the florist
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's meaningful repetition implies a romantic/personal connection to the florist — the comedy is in what's unsaid and the long pause implied
jokes.explanation: Economy is perfect — the repetition does all the work. The implication is clear and the deadpan delivery makes it funnier. A well-known structure (the meaningful double-statement) executed with precision.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: If this ceiling were to collapse right now, I'd be fine. I'd just walk out of here.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The rest of you, I don't know. But me? I'd be okay.

jokes.text: Yes! Ronnie! That's great! Thank you so much. / I'm doing this for David.
jokes.setup: Johnny thanks Ronnie effusively for using her connection to the florist
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's flat 'I'm doing this for David' — refusing to let Johnny take any credit or warmth from the gesture
jokes.explanation: Ronnie's characteristic bluntness — refusing to give Johnny any credit — is good character comedy. The 'Of course you are' response implies Johnny is slightly wounded, which adds a layer.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Are you wearing white?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, it's a beautiful Valentino gown.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You can't wear white to a wedding. You're not the bride.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, technically, you're the bride.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm the groom, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, so it's fine.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, it is not fine. You need to change.

jokes.text: Yeah, I guess we could use the garage for parking. We just have to keep a space clear in case Gwen comes back.
jokes.setup: The group is organizing logistics for the new venue
jokes.punchline: Bob casually mentions keeping a space for 'Gwen' — implying his wife may have left him but he's keeping her parking spot just in case, delivered with complete normalcy
jokes.explanation: A darkly funny throwaway that implies a whole tragic domestic situation in seven words. The comedy is in what's NOT said — Bob's complete emotional normalcy about a potentially devastating circumstance.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The dress came with a veil.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're wearing an actual wedding dress? With a veil?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, yes, that's what I just said.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you're literally wearing a complete wedding ensemble to go to the grocery store.

jokes.text: I mean, David's gonna have a heart attack, but it... it could work.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Town Hall as the venue
jokes.punchline: Johnny's practical assessment: David will be medically destabilized, but logistically it's fine
jokes.explanation: A functional joke that works because it's a perfectly accurate prediction of David's reaction — treated as a minor inconvenience rather than a reason to change plans.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. People are going to think we're getting married to each other.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? No, they're not.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, they are. You're in a wedding dress, walking me down the aisle. That's... that's a couple's entrance.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's not a couple's entrance, David. I'm your sister.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, and everyone here knows that, but to a casual observer, this looks deeply inappropriate.

jokes.text: You are shockingly calm for David Rose on his wedding day.
jokes.setup: Patrick calls David to tell him about the Town Hall venue, bracing for an explosion
jokes.punchline: David is inexplicably serene — the opposite of every expectation — which Patrick immediately notes with genuine shock
jokes.explanation: The comedic value is in the subverted expectation — after all the David panic, the massage has rendered him almost unrecognizable. Sets up the happy ending reveal.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's fine, it's not a big deal. We'll just... we'll keep some distance.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, we're literally going to be standing next to each other.

jokes.text: An indoor parking garage? / Those were all full.
jokes.setup: David calmly asks about the new venue
jokes.punchline: His first guess — indoor parking garage — is again hilariously specific; Patrick's deadpan 'those were all full' treats it as a serious alternative that was legitimately researched
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback and comedic escalation. The indoor parking garage is a funnier venue than Bob's Garage, and Patrick playing it completely straight — 'those were all full' — implies he actually looked into it. The deadpan commitment to the absurd premise makes this land very well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And another thing—the seamstress said the train needs to be reinforced because of the weight of the beading, which means we might need to adjust the bustle mechanism, and I'm not even sure if the original design accounted for—

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David. The dress is beautiful. We're not talking about this anymore.

jokes.text: Just because I didn't have the Town Hall on my mood board doesn't mean we still can't have fun.
jokes.setup: David is being remarkably calm about the venue change to Town Hall
jokes.punchline: He casually reveals he had a 'mood board' for his wedding — of course he did — and Town Hall wasn't on it, delivered with zen acceptance
jokes.explanation: The mood board detail is pure David Rose — of course he has a wedding mood board. The resigned acceptance that Town Hall wasn't on it, but they'll try anyway, is funny because it's such an understated capitulation from someone who cares deeply about aesthetics.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I feel terrible about ruining your wedding.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it was already ruined before you helped.

jokes.text: Wow. That massage really did a number, huh? / Uh, yeah. That was the first time I've ever received a happy ending.
jokes.setup: Patrick is surprised by David's unusual calm; David attributes it to the massage
jokes.punchline: 'That was the first time I've ever received a happy ending' — David uses the phrase completely innocently to mean his stress was resolved, not knowing (or not meaning) the sexual connotation
jokes.explanation: The inciting joke of the episode's funniest sequence. The misdirection is perfectly executed: David's sincere, innocent delivery of 'happy ending' sends the scene into chaos. The episode title itself is the punchline. Structure is near-perfect — the setup (David's unusual calm, Patrick's question) makes the line land perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Wedding Guest 1
dialogue.line: Oh, they look so happy together!

dialogue.speaker: Wedding Guest 2
dialogue.line: I always knew those two would end up together.

dialogue.speaker: Wedding Guest 1
dialogue.line: The way they're walking down the aisle... it's so romantic.

dialogue.speaker: Wedding Guest 3
dialogue.line: Wait, I thought David was marrying Patrick?

dialogue.speaker: Wedding Guest 2
dialogue.line: Shh, just enjoy the moment.

jokes.text: Sorry, what did you just say? / The happy ending that the masseur gave me. I've always heard of things like that, but I've never actually gotten one.
jokes.setup: David has just said he received a 'happy ending' from his massage
jokes.punchline: Patrick's stunned 'sorry, what?' prompts David to elaborate — making it worse — with 'I've always heard of things like that, but I've never actually gotten one'
jokes.explanation: The escalation makes the original joke even better. David's clarification digs the hole deeper while still being completely sincere. 'I've always heard of things like that' implies he thought masseurs routinely did this — the innocence is the engine of the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Love is like a baby crow. It's awkward, it's not very attractive, and you're not entirely sure it's going to make it.

jokes.text: Did you have to pay extra for that?
jokes.setup: Patrick is processing that David got a 'happy ending' from a masseur
jokes.punchline: Patrick's brain, apparently, goes immediately to economics — his first question is about the surcharge
jokes.explanation: Patrick's reaction being 'did you pay extra?' rather than jealousy or outrage is a perfect character choice — and funnier than either of those options. The economy is superb. It escalates the absurdity while revealing Patrick's practical mind even in crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And so as we gather here today, in this moment of profound significance, I find myself reflecting on the nature of love itself, which is much like a river, flowing endlessly through the landscape of our—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad. Dad, we're good. Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But David, I haven't even gotten to the part about—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I know. And I appreciate it, but we need to move things along.

jokes.text: I didn't pay for a happy ending! I paid for a massage! He gave you a happy ending?! On our wedding day?!
jokes.setup: Patrick asks if David paid extra; David protests his innocence
jokes.punchline: The realization cascades: David insisting he paid for a 'massage' while Patrick's 'He gave you a happy ending?! On our WEDDING DAY?!' escalates the indignation
jokes.explanation: The 'on our wedding day?!' indignation is perfect Patrick comedy. He's not upset about the act as much as the timing. The cross-purpose arguing (David denying payment, Patrick emphasizing the occasion) creates great comic rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And when I think about the future, I think about you, and I think about... actually, I need to be clear about something. Earlier today, when I said I was getting a massage, I meant a therapeutic massage. A professional massage. I was not, in any way, implying that I needed to be... worked on. Manually. By another person. Because I'm with you now, and you're the only person I want to—

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, I know what you meant.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you? Because your face suggested otherwise for a solid forty-five minutes.

jokes.text: I thought you wanted that?! / Why would I want you to get a happy ending from a stranger on our wedding day, David? / You tell me!
jokes.setup: Patrick is incredulous that David let the happy ending happen
jokes.punchline: David's 'I thought you wanted that?!' implies Patrick requested it, followed by the equally absurd 'you tell me!' when Patrick asks why he would want that
jokes.explanation: The 'YOU TELL ME!' is a great moment of David's defensive logic collapsing on itself. He can't explain why Patrick would want this but doubles down anyway. The absurdist circularity of the argument is classic David.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A son's wedding is hardly the time to become maudlin. One must remain composed, collected, and entirely emotionally unavailable.

jokes.text: See, this is what Ray should be filming.
jokes.setup: Patrick and David are having an absurd argument about the happy ending; Stevie and others are present
jokes.punchline: Stevie dryly notes that THIS is the moment that should be captured on film — referencing the earlier joke about Ray not filming the crisis planning
jokes.explanation: A perfect Stevie observation — her calm detachment from the chaos and ability to find the absurdity is well-deployed. The callback to Ray filming makes it better.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm going to miss you guys. This place, all of it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're drunk.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not drunk.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're tired then.

jokes.text: Okay, Marcia Clark? Court's adjourned for the day.
jokes.setup: Patrick is cross-examining David about whether he stopped the happy ending from happening
jokes.punchline: David shuts down the interrogation by calling Patrick 'Marcia Clark' — the O.J. Simpson trial prosecutor — and declaring court adjourned
jokes.explanation: A sharp pop-culture reference with genuine wit behind it — Marcia Clark as a prosecutor is specific and fitting for Patrick's cross-examination behavior. 'Court's adjourned for the day' is a good topper. The reference feels earned rather than gratuitous.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You smell like a distillery.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I didn't sleep. I just partied.

jokes.text: And you told me that you wanted to de-stress me. I just assumed you knew what you were paying for! I was both shocked and impressed!
jokes.setup: David continues defending his acceptance of the happy ending
jokes.punchline: 'I was both shocked and impressed!' is the perfect topper — David admits he was surprised AND complimentary of the masseur's work
jokes.explanation: The highest quotability score in the episode. 'I was both shocked and impressed!' as David's summary of receiving an unexpected happy ending from a wedding-day masseur is perfect. It's not a defense, not an apology — it's an aesthetic review. David Rose would absolutely rate the quality of an unexpected sexual experience on his own wedding day. This line will outlive the episode. The 'both' doing structural work to separate his two emotional responses is the final touch.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Now, when you ship the girls, I want them in individual climate-controlled boxes with their own security detail.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Your wigs need a security detail?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: These aren't just wigs, Johnny. These are the girls. They've been with me through three marriages, two bankruptcies, and a complete loss of cellular service in Schitt's Creek. They deserve to be treated with the utmost care and respect.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'll... make a note of that.

jokes.text: All I did was leave an envelope full of cash and a note that said to take very good care of you. And now that I've said those words out loud, I can see how a certain kind of person would interpret that the wrong way.
jokes.setup: Patrick explains what instructions he left for the masseur
jokes.punchline: As Patrick speaks the words aloud — 'envelope full of cash... take very good care of you' — he realizes in real time that he accidentally hired a sex worker for David on their wedding day
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The structure is exceptional: Patrick explaining his entirely innocent intentions only to hear himself say 'envelope full of cash... take very good care of you' and realizing the terrible implication IN REAL TIME is comedy perfection. 'A certain kind of person' as the euphemism for an adult service industry client is perfectly chosen. The earned-vs-cheap score is near-perfect because the whole sequence was set up honestly and this punchline was completely earned through character logic. This is comparable to the anchor examples for 8+ quality.

jokes.text: Okay, but was it good at least? / Do not answer that. Was it? Don't.
jokes.setup: The happy ending situation has been revealed and explained
jokes.punchline: Stevie, who has been observing, asks the only logical next question ('was it good?'); Patrick immediately vetoes it but then can't help asking himself
jokes.explanation: An outstanding topper to the sequence. Three beats working perfectly: (1) Stevie asking the question everyone is thinking, (2) Patrick shutting it down, (3) Patrick immediately asking it himself. 'Do not answer that. Was it? Don't.' is a beautifully compressed three-beat joke that reveals Patrick's conflict. Highly quotable and gets a huge laugh from the room.

jokes.text: I think Stevie and I should probably go get ready. Leave you to put your little suit on. Nothing compares to you. My husband-to-be.
jokes.setup: After the happy ending debacle, Patrick needs to exit gracefully
jokes.punchline: His departure is a collection of loving non-sequiturs: 'your little suit,' 'nothing compares to you' — both tender and dismissive simultaneously
jokes.explanation: 'Your little suit' is a funny and affectionate detail; 'nothing compares to you' as a sincere and somewhat absurd declaration post-happy-ending scandal gives the scene a warm landing.

jokes.text: Oh my God. [reaction beat after Patrick and Stevie leave]
jokes.setup: Patrick and Stevie have just left after the happy ending revelation
jokes.punchline: David's quiet 'Oh my God' — alone, processing what just happened on his wedding morning
jokes.explanation: A button on the sequence — David's processing of the chaos he's created. Works as a comedic exhale after the escalation.

jokes.text: Something can fall from the ceiling and just go Bffggghhh! I'm dead! / Not me though, I'm prepared.
jokes.setup: Roland is giving a speech about Johnny leaving town and the preciousness of life
jokes.punchline: Roland demonstrates a hypothetical death by falling ceiling debris with a sound effect, then immediately exempts himself because he's 'prepared'
jokes.explanation: Pure Roland comedy — his mortality monologue derails into a death demonstration with sound effects. 'Not me though, I'm prepared' as a tag is perfectly Roland's self-importance. The sound effect ('Bffggghhh!') works as physical absurdism even in text form.

jokes.text: And according to the wedding schedule, you're supposed to be dressed and ready to greet me. Greet you?
jokes.setup: David arrives to find Alexis not prepared to walk him down the aisle
jokes.punchline: David expects to be 'greeted' per his wedding schedule; Alexis's incredulous 'Greet you?' reveals she had no idea this was a thing
jokes.explanation: The detail that David has a 'wedding schedule' with greeting protocols is funny, and Alexis's bafflement is a good character reaction.

jokes.text: You look very expensive and gorgeous too. / It's-it's white. You're wearing a white dress.
jokes.setup: David and Alexis compliment each other — David is in his wedding suit; Alexis is in what she considers appropriate attire
jokes.punchline: David notices Alexis is wearing white — potentially upstaging him at his own wedding — his horror is the punchline
jokes.explanation: Classic sibling comedy — Alexis's oblivious style choice creating a crisis for David. The 'you look very expensive' compliment followed immediately by horror is a good comedic beat.

jokes.text: Duh. It's black and white themed. What was I supposed to show up in pink? / Or black! You're wearing a wedding dress to my wedding!
jokes.setup: David is horrified that Alexis is wearing white
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'Duh, it's black and white themed' is delivered with perfect Alexis logic; David's 'or BLACK!' reveals the obvious alternative she missed
jokes.explanation: Alexis's complete sincerity about the logic ('it's black and white themed!') makes the joke work. David pointing out the obvious alternative — she could have worn the other color in the theme — is a perfectly structured retort.

jokes.text: This is not a wedding dress. It's a white, floor-length gown. It's very different. / Did it come with a veil? / No. It came with a headdress.
jokes.setup: Alexis insists her white floor-length gown is not a wedding dress
jokes.punchline: David's perfectly targeted question ('did it come with a veil?') is answered with 'No. It came with a headdress.' — which somehow makes it worse
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is perfect: David tests the wedding dress hypothesis with the most targeted possible question (veil), gets denied — but then the answer (headdress) is somehow worse. The joke escalates rather than resolves. Alexis's complete sincerity ('It came with a headdress') delivered as a distinction rather than a condemnation is inspired character writing.

jokes.text: You're walking me down the aisle in a wedding dress. Everyone is gonna think we're getting married to each other.
jokes.setup: Alexis has revealed the dress came with a headdress she chose not to wear
jokes.punchline: David's horror reaches its peak: the combination of white dress + him in a suit + walking the aisle = they'll look like a couple getting married
jokes.explanation: A logical escalation of the Alexis-dress situation that works because it's true. The absurdity of David and Alexis visually appearing to marry each other is funny, though the joke is somewhat telegraphed.

jokes.text: Now, Stevie, as my Maid-of-Honour, does it not look like Alexis is wearing a wedding dress? Or just like a dress that happens to be white? / I don't wanna get involved.
jokes.setup: David tries to get Stevie to arbitrate the wedding dress dispute
jokes.punchline: Stevie flatly refuses to take sides: 'I don't wanna get involved' — exactly the response of anyone who knows both David and Alexis
jokes.explanation: Stevie's self-preservation instinct ('I don't wanna get involved') is perfectly in character. The absurdity of invoking the Maid-of-Honour title to force a fashion verdict makes her refusal funnier.

jokes.text: So much for the massage.
jokes.setup: David is spiraling about the wedding dress situation despite the massage meant to calm him
jokes.punchline: Stevie's dry callback to the massage — and by extension, the happy ending — lands with maximum economy and multiple layers of meaning
jokes.explanation: Four words doing the work of three scenes. The callback is perfectly timed — Stevie, who witnessed the happy ending drama, gets to land the callback with maximum economy. The line means both 'the massage didn't calm him' and 'despite everything that happened in that massage room.' Rewatch value high.

jokes.text: You don't have a choice. You work for me today. / So much for the massage.
jokes.setup: Stevie refuses to arbitrate the wedding dress dispute
jokes.punchline: David insists she works for him as Maid-of-Honour; Stevie's 'so much for the massage' is a callback to David's zen state being destroyed
jokes.explanation: Stevie's dry callback to the massage — noting that the calm didn't survive contact with Alexis's dress — is a sharp comedic observation. 'So much for the massage' is economical and Stevie-accurate.

jokes.text: I don't think you should worry about it. I'm sure this town has seen far weirder things.
jokes.setup: David is panicking about Alexis appearing to be in a wedding dress for the processional
jokes.punchline: Stevie's reassurance — 'I'm sure this town has seen far weirder things' — implies Schitt's Creek has a rich history of public weirdness that makes Alexis's outfit unremarkable
jokes.explanation: Stevie's dry reassurance works because of the implication — Schitt's Creek is a town where stranger things than a brother being walked down the aisle by a sister in a white gown have happened. The show's entire premise is in that subtext. Functional and warm.

jokes.text: Is that a bow in the back? / Don't, David.
jokes.setup: David is still scrutinizing Alexis's dress after being told to drop it
jokes.punchline: He spots a bow on the back of the dress — further wedding dress evidence — Alexis's flat 'Don't, David' is a pre-emptive shutdown of the spiral
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the dress sequence. David can't help himself — his eye catches the bow — and Alexis's 'Don't, David' pre-empts the escalation she knows is coming. Two words that shut down a whole argument. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Right, Stevie? Right? / Hm.
jokes.setup: Alexis enthusiastically affirms that they love David and seeks Stevie's confirmation
jokes.punchline: Stevie's response is a single non-committal 'Hm' — neither confirming nor denying her love for David
jokes.explanation: Stevie's 'Hm' is the joke — she won't even commit to loving her best friend on his wedding day, which is both completely in character and deeply funny. The economy is absolute maximum (one syllable) and the character integration is perfect for Stevie's emotional reticence. The fact that this is clearly the most she'll say is the warmth underneath.

jokes.text: I think you're giving yourself a lot of credit. My wedding was already ruined.
jokes.setup: Alexis feels terrible about wearing the white dress and thinks she's ruining the wedding
jokes.punchline: David dismisses her self-blame by pointing out the wedding was already ruined — the dress is merely the latest in a long list of disasters
jokes.explanation: A nice comedic callback that summarizes the episode's disaster-chain. David being generous to Alexis by contextualizing the dress within the broader catastrophe is both funny and touching.

jokes.text: Is it me, or do they look like husband and wife? / Kind of.
jokes.setup: David (in dark suit) and Alexis (in white floor-length gown) walk down the aisle together
jokes.punchline: A wedding guest observes exactly what David predicted — they look like they're getting married to each other
jokes.explanation: The payoff to David's prediction is executed perfectly. The outside observer confirming his fear, with the guest's casual 'Kind of' as a hedge, is funnier than a full confirmation would be. The visual gag (Alexis in white walking a groom down the aisle) lands beautifully.

jokes.text: And David... / Brewer. / Brewer.
jokes.setup: Moira is officiating the wedding and goes to name the grooms
jokes.punchline: She forgets Patrick's last name and has to be corrected by the groom himself at the altar — at the most solemn moment of the ceremony
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira moment — she's so self-involved that she doesn't know her son's partner's last name, and must be corrected by Patrick himself in the middle of the ceremony. The economy is exceptional: three words (David... / Brewer. / Brewer.) contain a complete character joke. The repetition of 'Brewer' as Moira recovers without apology is a wonderful detail.

jokes.text: In this little town. In the middle of nowhere. Okay. I think we're good.
jokes.setup: Moira is building to a heartfelt conclusion about her family finding solid ground
jokes.punchline: After the beautiful build — 'this little town, in the middle of nowhere' — she abruptly deflates it with 'Okay. I think we're good.' as if checking off a box
jokes.explanation: The emotional whiplash is perfectly executed. Moira builds genuine warmth and then immediately punctures it with a brisk 'Okay. I think we're good.' — as if she's finished a task rather than delivered a speech. It's funny AND emotionally resonant, which is Schitt's Creek at its best.

jokes.text: And now I will ask David Rose and Patrick... / Brewer. / Brewer.
jokes.setup: Moira continues the ceremony
jokes.punchline: She forgets Patrick's last name this time — requiring another assist from Johnny — and then repeats it as though she knew it all along
jokes.explanation: The running gag of Moira requiring prompted last names now covers both groom's surnames — she forgot David's, now she's forgotten Patrick's. Johnny's consistent prompt (one word) and Moira's instant adoption as though she said it herself ('Brewer') is perfect. The pattern is funnier the second time. Only Moira could be this confidently incompetent.

jokes.text: Well, you did such a great job, sweetheart. I don't know how you do it. / Oh, someone has to hold it together? There's a time and place for sentimentality, and your only son's wedding day is hardly the moment.
jokes.setup: The ceremony is over; Johnny praises Moira for holding together
jokes.punchline: Moira explains that a son's wedding is NOT the time for sentimentality — delivered with complete conviction, despite it being the most obvious occasion for it
jokes.explanation: Moira's inverted emotional logic — 'your only son's wedding day is hardly the moment' for sentimentality — is excellent. It's dark, it's wrong, and it's completely Moira. The irony is rich: she just delivered a sincere ceremony speech but now claims immunity from feelings.

jokes.text: Yes, just as sweet as the liqueur wafting off your breath. / I also didn't go to bed last night.
jokes.setup: Stevie says she'll miss the Roses; Moira thanks her for the sweet sentiment
jokes.punchline: Moira notes the liqueur on Stevie's breath; Stevie's non-defensive 'I also didn't go to bed last night' is a perfect deadpan admission
jokes.explanation: A warm and funny final Stevie moment. 'I also didn't go to bed last night' delivered without shame or apology is perfectly Stevie. The 'also' implies others didn't sleep either, which adds a communal chaos layer.

jokes.text: And uh, if you get the chance, Room 7 needs a turndown.
jokes.setup: Johnny is saying his final goodbye to Stevie at the motel he has run for years
jokes.punchline: In his last words before leaving forever, he asks her to turn down Room 7 — reverting to motel manager mode as a way of expressing love through work
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quietly perfect jokes. Johnny's emotional defense mechanism — retreating into motel manager professionalism at the most sentimental possible moment — is deeply in character and genuinely moving. 'Room 7 needs a turndown' as a final goodbye is both funny and heartbreaking. The economy is exceptional. This is comparable to the 8.0 anchor joke in quality, and arguably surpasses it in emotional resonance.

jokes.text: Okay. Make sure the girls are shipped in a temperature controlled cargo container. / Temperature controlled cargo container.
jokes.setup: The Roses are packing up to leave; Moira's wigs ('the girls') need transport
jokes.punchline: Moira's concern about the climate-controlled transport of her wigs — referred to as 'the girls' — and Johnny's dutiful repetition as if confirming a shipping manifest
jokes.explanation: The wigs ('the girls') as precious cargo requiring temperature-controlled shipping is a long-running character joke for Moira. Johnny's deadpan repetition like a checklist is funny. Works well for fans of the series.

jokes.text: Make sure the girls are shipped in a... / Temperature controlled / Temperature controlled / cargo container. Aha. / Cargo container. Aha.
jokes.setup: The Roses are saying their final goodbyes and coordinating moving logistics
jokes.punchline: Moira and Johnny perfectly finish each other's sentences about shipping her wigs ('the girls') in a temperature-controlled cargo container — the absurdity of treating wigs with this level of logistical care
jokes.explanation: The synchronized finishing of each other's sentences about wig logistics shows how perfectly attuned Moira and Johnny are — they've had this conversation enough times that it's choreography. The comedy is both in the absurdity of the wig logistics AND in the warmth of their partnership. 'The girls' as a term for wigs requiring temperature-controlled shipping is perfect Moira.

tmdb_overview: David stresses out over the rain on his wedding day, the family scrambles to make it right.
insight: Series finale sustains 85.6 score across 46 jokes with escalation and character beats.
